questions.prompt: Why has CSS-in-JS (styled-components, Emotion) declined in popularity?

choices.text: Performance is the same as everything else.

Multiple reasons compounded. Runtime cost — most CSS-in-JS libraries inject styles at render, creating measurable performance overhead at scale. SSR complications — generating styles server-side adds setup. Critically, React Server Components don't work well with most CSS-in-JS libraries (they require runtime that doesn't fit RSC's serialization model). As Next.js App Router with RSC became dominant, CSS-in-JS lost ground rapidly. Bundle size also adds 10-15KB. Tailwind generates static CSS (zero runtime), works perfectly with RSC, has no bundle overhead beyond the utility CSS itself.

questions.explanation: Runtime cost, SSR complications, doesn't fit React Server Components. Tailwind sidesteps all three.

questions.prompt: Existing CSS Modules codebase, team is productive. Migrate to Tailwind?

choices.text: Not unless you have a real pain point. A working CSS Modules codebase is better than a half-migrated one with two systems fighting. Migrate when there's actual friction (design inconsistency creeping in, slow style iteration, team frustration with naming/refactoring) — solve THAT problem. If the team is productive and the existing CSS works, switching for fashion is net-negative: months of migration cost, team disruption, no real win. For new projects: Tailwind. For working CSS Modules projects: only migrate when the pain justifies the work.

questions.explanation: Working code is better than half-migrated code. Switch when there's a real pain point, not for fashion.
